question: What are the key visual symptoms of guava canker on leaves?
ground_truth: Guava canker caused by Pestalotiopsis psidii presents as dark brown to black necrotic spots with a lighter tan centre, surrounded by a yellow chlorotic halo. Lesions begin as small water-soaked spots (1-3mm) that enlarge to 5-15mm irregular lesions. Pycnidia (tiny black fruiting bodies) are visible on the central necrotic zone. Lesions coalesce in severe infections causing blight and defoliation.

question: Which fungicides are most effective against guava canker and at what concentrations?
ground_truth: Carbendazim 50% WP at 0.1% (1g/L) is the primary systemic fungicide for guava canker. Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 0.3% (3g/L) is an effective contact protectant. Mancozeb 75% WP at 0.25% (2.5g/L) is used as a broad-spectrum protectant. Bordeaux mixture (1%) can be applied as a preventive spray. Applications should be made every 10-14 days during high disease pressure.

question: What biological or organic treatments are available for managing guava canker?
ground_truth: Trichoderma harzianum applied as a soil drench (5g/L) suppresses soilborne Pestalotiopsis inoculum. Bacillus subtilis foliar spray produces antibiotics (iturin, surfactin) active against fungal pathogens. Pseudomonas fluorescens (5g/L) can be used as a biocontrol drench. Bordeaux mixture (copper-lime) serves as a traditional organic protectant. Neem oil spray (2-3%) shows antifungal activity.

question: What environmental conditions favour the spread of guava canker?
ground_truth: Guava canker spreads most rapidly under warm temperatures (25-32°C) combined with high relative humidity (>80%) and prolonged leaf wetness from rainfall or overhead irrigation. Wind-driven rain disperses Pestalotiopsis spores from infected to healthy tissue. The monsoon season (June-September) is the peak risk period. Dense canopy with poor air circulation creates microclimates favourable to infection. Insect wounds and pruning cuts serve as primary entry points.

question: What cultural and preventive practices reduce guava canker incidence?
ground_truth: Key preventive practices include pruning to maintain an open canopy for air circulation, applying Bordeaux paste to all pruning cuts, switching to drip irrigation to reduce leaf wetness, removing and burning infected material promptly, sterilising pruning tools with 70% alcohol between cuts, applying copper oxychloride 0.3% before the monsoon season as a prophylactic spray, and maintaining balanced NPK nutrition to avoid plant stress.

question: What does a healthy guava leaf look like visually?
ground_truth: A healthy guava leaf shows uniform bright to deep green coloration with a smooth adaxial (upper) surface and slightly pubescent abaxial (lower) surface. The leaf is 7-15cm long with prominent pinnate venation (10-15 pairs of lateral veins), entire smooth margins, firm leathery texture, and emits a characteristic guava aroma when crushed. No lesions, spots, discoloration, holes, powdery deposits, or wilting should be present.

question: What is the recommended monthly preventive spray schedule for a healthy guava orchard?
ground_truth: The preventive spray calendar includes: February - Bordeaux mixture 0.5% (pre-blossom protection); April-May - Copper oxychloride 0.3% (pre-monsoon); June - Mancozeb 0.25% (monsoon disease prevention); July-August - Copper oxychloride 0.3% (monsoon continuation); September - Neem oil 2ml/L + Mancozeb 0.25% (pest and disease combined); October - Propiconazole 1ml/L (rust prevention post-monsoon flush); December-January - Micronutrient spray (Zinc + Boron + Iron).

question: What biological products support health maintenance in a guava orchard?
ground_truth: Biological maintenance includes Pseudomonas fluorescens (5g/L) as soil drench twice yearly to promote root health and suppress soilborne pathogens, Trichoderma harzianum soil drenches to build soilborne disease suppression, neem oil (2ml/L) monthly as a broad-spectrum organic protectant, and conservation of natural predators like Chrysoperla carnea (green lacewing) and Cryptolaemus montrouzieri (mealybug destroyer) in the orchard.

question: What conditions must be maintained to keep guava plants healthy long-term?
ground_truth: Guava thrives at 20-35°C with 1000-2000mm annual rainfall or supplemental irrigation. Optimal soil pH is 6.0-7.5 in well-drained sandy loam to loam soil with minimum 60cm depth. Regular drip irrigation every 3-5 days in summer prevents drought stress. Balanced NPK fertilization (260-500g N, 250-350g P, 300-400g K per tree per year) with annual micronutrient supplementation (Zn, B, Fe) maintains plant immunity and vigour.

question: What weekly monitoring practices should farmers follow for a healthy guava orchard?
ground_truth: Weekly monitoring should include: inspecting leaf undersides for mealybug colonies (white cottony masses), thrips (tiny insects in folded margins), and aphid clusters on young shoots; checking new growth flushes for early disease signs; monitoring yellow and blue sticky traps for pest population trends; examining fruit for oviposition punctures from fruit flies; checking soil moisture and irrigation adequacy; and maintaining orchard floor cleanliness by removing fallen leaves and mummified fruits.

question: How do you distinguish thrips damage from mealybug damage on guava leaves?
ground_truth: Thrips damage appears as silver-grey to bronze streaking on the upper leaf surface with downward curling of leaf margins, creating a silvery sheen on new growth. Thrips are tiny (1-2mm) pale yellow to dark brown insects found in folded leaf margins. Mealybug damage presents as white cottony waxy masses on leaf undersides and shoot junctions, with yellowing, honeydew secretion leading to black sooty mold, and ants tending the colonies. Thrips cause rasping injury; mealybugs cause phloem-sucking yellowing.

question: What insecticides are recommended for thrips control in guava?
ground_truth: For thrips control: Spinosad 45% SC at 0.5ml/L is highly effective with low mammalian toxicity; Imidacloprid 70% WG at 0.3g/L applied as foliar spray or soil drench provides systemic control; Fipronil 5% SC at 1.5ml/L is reserved for severe infestations but is toxic to beneficial insects. Pre-harvest intervals of 14-21 days must be observed. For mealybug: Buprofezin 25% SC at 1.5ml/L (insect growth regulator, safe for beneficials) or Profenofos 50% EC at 2ml/L for heavy infestations.

question: What biocontrol agents are effective against guava insect pests?
ground_truth: Key biocontrol agents include: Cryptolaemus montrouzieri (mealybug destroyer beetle) - release 5-10 adults per infested tree; Chrysoperla carnea (green lacewing) - release 1000-2000 eggs per acre against aphids, thrips, and mealybug; Beauveria bassiana at 5g/L applied in the evening against thrips, mealybug, and leafhoppers; Anagyrus lopezi parasitic wasps for mealybug suppression; and Isaria fumosorosea for whiteflies and thrips.

question: During which seasons are guava insect pests most active and why?
ground_truth: Thrips and mealybugs peak during hot, dry weather (March-May) when plants produce soft, succulent new flushes preferred by sucking insects. Aphids peak in cool, dry weather. Fruit flies peak during monsoon and fruiting season (June-August) when ripening fruits are available for oviposition. Leafhoppers are active year-round but increase during warm, humid periods. Excessive nitrogen fertilization, which promotes soft tissue, and disruption of natural predator populations by broad-spectrum insecticides trigger outbreaks.

question: What IPM cultural practices prevent insect pest outbreaks in guava orchards?
ground_truth: IPM cultural practices include: installing yellow sticky traps (2-4 per acre) for thrips and aphid monitoring, blue sticky traps (5 per acre) for thrips, and methyl eugenol traps (5 per acre) for fruit fly monitoring; applying kaolin clay spray (5% suspension) as a physical barrier deterring thrips and fruit flies; controlling ant colonies with sticky trunk barriers to protect natural enemies of mealybugs and aphids; avoiding excess nitrogen fertilization; pruning dense canopy to reduce pest shelter; and removing weeds that serve as alternate hosts.

question: What does mummified guava fruit look like and how does it differ from normal fruit drop?
ground_truth: Mummified guava fruits fail to develop, turning from pale green to dark brown to black over 2-4 weeks after fruit set. The fruits become hard, leathery, completely desiccated, shrivelling to 20-30% of normal size. They either hang on the tree for weeks or drop prematurely. Key differentiators from normal fruit drop: mummified fruits remain hard and dried (not soft), surface may show small black pycnidia or fruiting bodies of the causal fungus, and under humid conditions salmon-pink to orange spore masses may be visible from Colletotrichum sporulation.

question: Which fungicides control fungal mummification in guava and when should they be applied?
ground_truth: Application schedule for fungal mummification: Spray 1 at 50% flowering - Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 0.3% or Mancozeb 75% WP at 0.25% (protectant); Spray 2 at fruit set (pea stage) - Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/L or Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/L (systemic); Spray 3 fifteen days later if disease persists - Tebuconazole 25.9% EC at 1ml/L (rotation of mode of action). Wound treatment: apply Bordeaux paste or Copper oxychloride paste to all pruning cuts on infected shoots.

question: How does Trichoderma and Bacillus subtilis help manage guava mummification?
ground_truth: Trichoderma harzianum soil drench (5g/L) suppresses soilborne Colletotrichum and Botryodiplodia spore populations that cause mummification. Bacillus subtilis foliar spray produces iturin and surfactin antibiotics that are effective against Colletotrichum and Pestalotiopsis psidii, the primary biotic mummification agents. These biocontrol agents are applied between chemical fungicide sprays to build a layered defense without resistance development. Orchard biodiversity conservation also supports beneficial predatory fungi.

question: What conditions favour biotic mummification compared to abiotic mummification in guava?
ground_truth: Biotic mummification is favoured by high rainfall during flowering and early fruit set, temperatures of 25-32°C, relative humidity above 80%, and previous season inoculum in the form of mummified fruits left on the tree. Abiotic mummification is triggered by severe drought stress, extreme heat, boron deficiency, or calcium deficiency. The key differentiator: biotic mummification shows fungal fruiting bodies (pycnidia, sporodochia) and occurs in a scattered pattern, while abiotic mummification affects sun-exposed sides uniformly with no fungal bodies visible.

question: What is the single most important cultural practice to prevent mummification recurrence?
ground_truth: Removing ALL mummified fruits and dead twigs from the previous season BEFORE new flowering begins is the single most important management practice, as mummified fruits are primary inoculum reservoirs that sporulate and infect new growth. Additional key practices: collect and destroy fallen mummified fruits weekly, apply Bordeaux paste to all pruning cut surfaces, improve soil drainage to reduce Botryodiplodia and Phomopsis activity, apply borax 0.3% foliar spray before flowering to prevent boron deficiency-induced physiological mummification, and ensure balanced NPK + calcium nutrition.

question: What visual symptom patterns indicate multiple concurrent diseases on a guava leaf?
ground_truth: Multiple disease complex shows concurrent symptom types: dark brown-black necrotic lesions (Pestalotiopsis canker) co-occurring with bright orange-yellow powdery pustules (Puccinia rust) in post-monsoon period; irregular feeding holes or silvery thrips streaks surrounding expanding brown necrotic zones where insect wounds have allowed fungal entry; marginal browning (abiotic scorch) combined with scattered fungal spots; or simultaneous young fruit mummification with leaf canker lesions and insect feeding damage. The presence of two or more distinct symptom types on the same leaf indicates multiple disease complex.

question: What is the 4-week chemical treatment schedule for multiple disease complex in guava?
ground_truth: Week 1 (Fungicide priority): If rust present - Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/L (systemic triazole); if canker/anthracnose dominant - Carbendazim 50% WP at 1g/L + Mancozeb 75% WP at 2.5g/L tank mix. Week 2 (Insecticide, 3-4 days after fungicide): Imidacloprid 70% WG at 0.3g/L or Thiamethoxam 25% WG at 0.2g/L for sucking pests; Chlorpyrifos 20% EC at 2ml/L for chewing insects (avoid during flowering). Week 3 (Nutrition): Zinc sulfate 0.3% + Boron 0.2% + Magnesium sulfate 0.5% foliar spray. Week 4 (Rotation fungicide): Tebuconazole 25.9% EC at 1ml/L.

question: Which combination of biological agents is recommended for multiple disease complex in guava?
ground_truth: For multiple disease complex, a layered biological approach is recommended: Beauveria bassiana 5g/L (evening application) controls thrips, mealybug, and whitefly; Bacillus subtilis 5g/L provides broad antifungal activity against the fungal components and is applied between chemical fungicide sprays; Chrysoperla carnea (green lacewing) eggs at 1000-2000 per acre target soft-bodied insects; Trichoderma harzianum soil drench (5g/L) builds soilborne disease suppression. Potassium Silicate soil drench (2ml/L) strengthens cell walls and reduces fungal penetration.

question: What predisposing factors cause a guava plant to develop multiple concurrent diseases?
ground_truth: Key predisposing factors for multiple disease complex: prolonged drought followed by sudden heavy rain (cracks cell walls and activates multiple pathogens simultaneously); excessive nitrogen fertilization (promotes succulent tissue favoured by both sucking insects and fungi); dense unmanaged canopy (high internal humidity, poor spray penetration); neglected pruning (accumulated dead wood harbours multiple pathogen inocula); soil waterlogging (root stress causing systemic immune suppression); neonicotinoid overuse (destroys natural predator populations enabling pest-mediated pathogen spread); and micronutrient deficiency of Zn, B, or Ca (weakens cell wall integrity and reduces plant defence compounds).

question: What 3-month orchard rehabilitation plan is recommended after a multiple disease episode?
ground_truth: The 3-month rehabilitation plan: Weeks 1-2 - Sanitation (remove all infected material, sterilize tools); Weeks 3-4 - Chemical spray protocol (fungicide + insecticide as per 4-week schedule); Weeks 5-6 - Balanced NPK + micronutrient application; Weeks 7-8 - Biological soil drenches (Trichoderma + Pseudomonas fluorescens); Weeks 9-10 - Canopy restructuring (pruning for better air circulation, Bordeaux paste on cuts); Weeks 11-12 - Monitoring setup (weekly scouting, pest traps installation, record-keeping). Future prevention: fortnightly preventive spray calendar, year-round IPM traps, annual soil testing.

question: What are the distinguishing visual features of guava rust and how does it progress?
ground_truth: Guava rust (Puccinia psidii) begins with small pale yellow spots (1-3mm) on the upper leaf surface with corresponding pale greenish-yellow uredia on the lower surface (Days 1-5). Active sporulation (Days 5-14) shows bright orange to yellow-orange powdery pustules (uredinia) on the lower leaf surface - touching them leaves orange powder on hands. The upper surface shows corresponding yellow to orange spots with chlorotic surrounding tissue. Advanced stage: pustules coalesce into large orange patches, massive defoliation within 2-3 weeks, dieback of young shoots. This bright orange powdery appearance on leaf undersides is the pathognomonic feature of rust.

question: Which triazole fungicides are most effective against guava rust and how should they be rotated?
ground_truth: Propiconazole 25% EC at 1ml/L is the first-line systemic triazole for guava rust - highly effective and should be applied at the first sign of orange pustules. Tebuconazole 25.9% EC at 1ml/L is an excellent alternative systemic triazole. For resistance management, rotate triazoles (Propiconazole, Tebuconazole) with contact protectants: Mancozeb 75% WP at 0.25% or Copper oxychloride 50% WP at 0.3%. Apply at 10-14 day intervals. Wettable Sulfur 80% WP at 2.5g/L is effective at early stages but must be avoided above 32°C to prevent phytotoxicity.

question: What organic and biological options exist for guava rust management?
ground_truth: Wettable Sulfur 80% WP at 2.5g/L is the primary organic option effective at early rust stages - avoid in hot weather above 32°C to prevent phytotoxicity. Trichoderma soil drenches are not directly effective against rust but improve overall plant health and resistance. Salicylic acid spray (0.1-0.5mM) has been reported in research settings to activate plant Systemic Acquired Resistance (SAR) against Puccinia psidii. Maintaining orchard biodiversity and avoiding broad-spectrum pesticide overuse helps conserve natural fungal antagonists.

question: What temperature and humidity conditions favour guava rust infection?
ground_truth: Guava rust is optimally active at 18-25°C - a cool to warm temperature range. Infection is poor above 30°C, making it primarily a cool-season and post-monsoon disease. Relative humidity above 90% with free water or prolonged leaf wetness (minimum 6 hours) is required for urediniospore germination and infection. New vegetative flushes (3-10 days old) are most susceptible; mature leaves are largely resistant. The post-monsoon period (October-December) and winter flush are peak risk in India, particularly at higher altitudes (cooler temperatures, higher humidity).

question: How should nurseries prevent guava rust introduction and what field monitoring is recommended?
ground_truth: Nursery prevention: enforce strict quarantine - do not import planting material from rust-endemic regions without phytosanitary certification; maintain separate air circulation zones; inspect all incoming material. Field monitoring: train farm workers to recognise the distinctive orange powder symptom; check new growth flushes daily during cool humid weather; apply pre-flush preventive spray of Mancozeb 0.25% or Copper oxychloride 0.3% before new leaves emerge during post-monsoon risk periods; apply Propiconazole 1ml/L at first orange pustule observation. Report severe outbreaks to local agricultural department as rust has quarantine significance.

question: How do you differentiate potassium deficiency scorch from fungal disease lesions on guava leaves?
ground_truth: Potassium deficiency scorch begins on the OLDEST (lowest) leaves and progresses upward, showing brown papery margins and tips with interveinal chlorosis (yellow zones between green veins). There is NO defined dark border, yellow halo, or fungal fruiting bodies - the affected area grades smoothly from yellow to brown. Texture is dry, crisp, and fragile. Fungal lesions (Pestalotiopsis canker) instead show interior leaf spots with a dark border and yellow halo, visible pycnidia (black dots), and affect leaves regardless of age position. Scorch is NOT contagious; fungal lesions spread progressively.

question: What is the corrective treatment for potassium deficiency-induced leaf scorch in guava?
ground_truth: For potassium deficiency scorch: immediate foliar spray - Potassium nitrate (KNO3) 0.5% (5g/L water) applied 2-3 times at 10-day intervals for quick uptake. Soil application: Muriate of potash (MOP/KCl) or Potassium sulfate (SOP/K2SO4) at 300-400g per tree in split doses with irrigation. Potassium sulfate is preferred in saline soils to avoid adding chloride. For magnesium scorch: Magnesium sulfate (Epsom salt) 0.5% foliar spray, 3 applications at 10-day intervals. For iron deficiency scorch in alkaline soils: Ferrous sulfate 0.2% + citric acid 0.1% foliar spray or chelated iron (Fe-EDTA/EDDHA) at 1-2g/L.

question: How does root-knot nematode infestation cause leaf scorch in guava and how is it treated biologically?
ground_truth: Root-knot nematodes (Meloidogyne incognita) damage fine feeder roots, reducing the tree's water uptake capacity. This causes canopy scorch even when soil is adequately moist, because the hydraulic connection between roots and leaves is compromised. Biological treatment: Paecilomyces lilacinus soil drench (5g/L) is an entomopathogenic fungus effective against nematode juveniles and eggs. Trichoderma harzianum drench (5g/L) around the root zone promotes root recovery and suppresses soilborne pathogens. Organic matter additions (compost, FYM) improve soil biology and suppress nematode populations naturally.

question: Which season and soil conditions most commonly cause leaf scorch in guava in India?
ground_truth: Leaf scorch peaks during summer (April-June) when high evapotranspiration demand exceeds water supply, and during post-monsoon dry spells (October-November) when sudden moisture deficit follows a long wet season. Sandy soils are high risk due to poor water and potassium retention and high leaching. Alkaline soils (pH above 7.5) cause iron, manganese, zinc, and boron to become chemically unavailable, manifesting as deficiency scorch. High winds increase transpiration demand intensifying drought scorch. Root-knot nematode infestation in any season can cause scorch symptoms even with adequate irrigation.

question: What preventive agronomic practices minimise leaf scorch risk in guava orchards?
ground_truth: Key preventive practices: soil testing every 2 years to correct potassium, magnesium, and pH proactively; applying 10cm organic mulch around each tree base to reduce soil temperature, conserve moisture, and prevent drought scorch; annual potassium application (MOP or SOP at 300-400g per tree) even without visible symptoms since guava is a high-K-demand crop; regular drip irrigation scheduling to maintain soil above 50% field capacity; calibrating spray equipment to avoid pesticide or herbicide overdose causing chemical burn; annual nematode monitoring with root inspection and proactive Paecilomyces lilacinus application in nematode-prone soils.
